speaker: interviewer
text: The date is June 13th 1996. The survivor, Joseph Talpert. The interviewer, Linda Behr. The interview is in Johannesburg, South Africa. The language, English. My name is Linda Behr. I'm interviewing Joseph Talpert in Johannesburg, South Africa. The date is June 13, 1996. The language, English. What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Joseph Talpert.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, J-O-S-E-P-H T-A-L-P-E-R-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Identical.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a nickname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so far, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birthdate?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthdate is 21 September, 1916.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your age now?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the city and the country of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The city is London, and the county is England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joseph, you were born in London. Was your family from England?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my family had to leave Belgium due to the war in 1914, 1918. They were refugees in London, where they got married, and I was born at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in London?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was til the end of the war. Must be '18, 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all the refugees went back to the countries where they came. And my parents, at the time, went back to Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that their home country, Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was our home country, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what town did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Antwerp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you grow up in Antwerp?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the area that you lived in Antwerp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the area lived was near the municipal park. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: More or less central in town. What kind of area was it? Who lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a residential area. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, Antwerp is not such a big town, but this was a residential because there's some business areas. This wasn't exactly in the business area. It was more residential.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you live in a house or apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we lived in a apartment, a flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well it's usual flat you know? A few bedrooms, dining room, lounge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how it was furnished?

speaker: interviewee
text: The usual furniture. I wouldn't say anything particular. It's the furniture what-- what was the usual-- I wouldn't say anything-- I would have described it-- it was nicely furnished, but I wouldn't say it was extraordinary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a room on your own, or did you share it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had my own room, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything special that you remember in your room?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think anything in particular.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your parents. What were they like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were quite a pleasant couple, you know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Just having a bit of a problem. I don't know what you want to know exactly. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I would say they were well matched couple, I would call it loving couple That's all I can say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a good relationship with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, so far, no, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a partner in a clothing factory with two of my uncles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: The factory was in Antwerp. Well, the street is called [NON-ENGLISH] street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it far from where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was quite a distance in this southern part of Antwerp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he get to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: By car.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had his own car.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was-- Well, you could go-- in Antwerp they quite-- tram service is good and the bus service is good. Anyway, if not by car, you could always find other means of transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the customers? Who were your father's customers? Were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had customers all over the country, all over Belgium, also Luxembourg, but it's a little country on the border. And we were quite well known and well liked in the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have any special interests?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he liked the acting part. In shows, he liked to participate.

speaker: interviewer
text: In any particular groups? What kind of shows were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was rather Jewish group. Jewish acting.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother didn't take an act and at the early stage she used to work. At home, she was a diamond cutter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she worked at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had a-- well, in diamond trade, you can have a machine. She didn't work in a factory. We had a machine at home, and she used to do a bit of work there. At the early stage of their marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your mother have any particular interests or hobbies?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so, not particular.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have brothers and sisters? I had a brother, a little bit younger than me. Maximilian. Maxi. And what was he like?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a very pleasant youngster.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a close relationship with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had, rather, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do things together? Did you confide in each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wouldn't say so. Well, he had his own activity, and I had my own activity. I was active in the Zionist [?  ?] movement.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which movements were those?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we had a student group. And I used to take, also, a big interest in the [INAUDIBLE] where I was rather active.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joseph, what kind of child were you? Do you remember how you were? Were you extroverted? Did you have lots of friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had many friends. Quite a big range of friends. We used to go out together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you remember your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite a pleasant life. Only the war spoiled it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any incidents from your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to go and enter-- being on the river [NON-ENGLISH], we used to have those outings. There was a boat going to Holland. This was quite a big outing, what we had. You know, it was quite entertainment. We got to go a town in Holland called [NON-ENGLISH]. And then we had other outings around Antwerp where we used to go just over the river.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of games did you play?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I used to play tennis. And also, at a later stage, I used to play cards, bridge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joseph, how do you remember your school life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was quite a pleasant life, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went, first to municipal school, what was a little distance away from us, and then the [NON-ENGLISH], what they call-- that's the high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Jewish schools or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were Jewish schools, but I didn't go in that Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the schools that you went to were mixed schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: --was a government school, a municipal school, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was the education? Did you-- did you think you had a good education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, education was good. Of course, in Belgium, it's not like in South Africa. School is the whole day-- finish 4, 5 o'clock in the afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any ambitions at that time? What did you think you would like to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be quite candid, I had no particular ambition. I never made up my mind what I'm going to do. And then war came, and everything was spoiled.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wouldn't call it religious, but we were observant-- what I would say, orthodox, conservative.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you spend the Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Jewish holidays we used to celebrate. Usually go to shul for the high holidays and you just observed the Jewish holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your home kosher?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, our home was kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a Jewish education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a Jewish education til my bar mitzvah. What I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: --called-- was a type cheder what we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Antwerp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to-- to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --cheder class?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before school? After school?

speaker: interviewee
text: After school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your bar mitzvah? Can you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Afraid to say my memory is not so good, but I can describe it, because it was combined with a wedding-- my uncle's wedding. We had it combined. A brother of my mother. And the bar mitzvah was combined celebration with their wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: What family came? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you remember the members of family who came to your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, the whole family was there plus quite a lot of guests. It would be too long to describe because they were quite a lot of people. You had a big extended family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father had brothers and sisters and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my father had a brother, two sisters. And my mother, they had also few sisters and brother. And then, we had a lot of friends. Well, originally my family comes from Lithuania, Russia. Then-- I wouldn't say everybody was there, but at least the close family was there, plus many friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and where was the reception? [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in a small home in Antwerp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brother also have a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my brother was at the bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joseph, were your parents political in any way? Did they take an interest in political life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they talk about politics, what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well speak about politics, but the truth is they didn't take an active part in politics.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when Hitler came to power, do you remember discussions in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, oh, of course it's everybody. We were all upset about it, when it started, because the country was put on alert. Not, I'm afraid, not it help much but anyhow it was an alert already started 1939 already, and everybody was upset.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you ever experienced any anti-Semitism at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was anti-Semitism in Belgium like in any other country, I would imagine. There were some Flemish movements what were extreme right, and they were anti-Semitic, but I wouldn't say it was on an unbearable scale. There was a movement.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever experience anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not personally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your friends Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Non Jewish? Non Jewish, as well as Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were all mostly Jewish. I had a few friends who were not Jewish but the majority was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, friends were mostly Jewish. Here and there there's some business relation where they are not Jewish, but I would say we circulate in circles which were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And generally, how did you feel the relationship between Jews and non-Jews in Antwerp--

speaker: interviewee
text: In general, it was good. I understand that it changed only war time, when I wasn't there. Then people showed their real colors, their real feelings.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anti-Semitic feelings-- but it might be caused to some greed, you know, that-- that some people told that Jews has a better life. And I think this was one of the causes of anti-Semitism in Belgium. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family react when they when they heard about the actions against the Jews in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, nobody was really happy about the events happening there. But due to the fact that they had a war before, they never expected things to go to that extreme, because Germany occupied Belgium in 1914, '18, and there was nothing anti-Jewish at the time. Everybody was worried about the time, but then nobody could expect it would go to that extreme. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family ever talk about leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they never spoke about leaving. They spoke about the children-- they perhaps should leave. When things-- we have family in the United States, and some Jewish families way of thinking about-- not only thinking-- some were sending their children away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they send them to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the United States. And other countries also, but mainly United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the parents stayed behind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the parents stayed behind, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened with the progress of the war? What happened when--

speaker: interviewee
text: When war started?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when war started it was, of course, a-- a big departure immediately. Most people who could leave, left. They went to France, because this was the first place, where people talked about going, because we always thought that France was a very strong country, and it would be rather safe there. Belgium, as the way it went-- we could see that Belgium couldn't resist. Then there was a huge departure towards France as the war started. And Belgium was invaded the 10th of May, 1940, and immediately, people started leaving. In fact, my family and myself we all left at the 10th of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the 10th of May?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you left? Did you just close your house? Your father's business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we just closed. Well, bombardment started, and it didn't sound very good. Then perhaps that year was that it wouldn't last long. You know, we'd be able to come back, because we had confidence in the neighboring country, which was mainly France, that they should be able to intervene, and things might improve then.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. Only bare necessities.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: By car.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in the car with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had a few-- we had my parents. We had the cars from the business, and we traveled with the cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it the four of you in the car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in our car there was, yeah, four. That's right. We traveled together with our uncle. We were with this family also. We traveled in two cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which way did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went towards the north-- well, I would say northwards towards the France-- like Rouen, we went that way-- Bordeaux. And eventually, in France itself, there were already organization-- French was directing people which direction to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a lot of cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was quite a lot. Many cars on the road.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they all going towards France?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all going mainly to France. Well, there was no other country, because the other side would have been Germany. There was only one direction you could go. France, you could go further if you wanted, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to have travel documents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you didn't need travel documents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it easy to cross--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you had your identity paper, your passport and-- but even then, you didn't need to. Identity papers were sufficient.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it easy to cross the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was no problem. The border was open. They were just showing it-- a way, where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who showed you where to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: The French authorities.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they show you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when you came to France, they showed you-- they were directing which way to go in France. They wouldn't show you to go to Paris. They were showing you to go southwards, towards the south of France

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know where you were going to, in the south of France?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not exactly, but we were following the road-- the instructions of the of the gendarmerie, the police, the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your family's intention when you got to the south of France?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be quite candid, nobody had particular plans, because nobody expected things to go so bad-- turned much worse than expected.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And besides this, nobody had financial arrangements to go immediately further, because everything was left behind. At the [INAUDIBLE] when it started in the morning, the banks opened-- was a mad rush. People taking some money out, but nobody knew how much you had to take out. Nobody was prepared for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in France, we eventually landed in Toulouse. And there, stayed sometime. Of course, Belgium was occupied, at the time. And-- didn't sound so bad. The rumors were the German behaved well. They did behave well at the begin. Then eventually, my parents, my brother, and my-- another part of the family [INAUDIBLE], they went bank to Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they left everything behind, and they were told things were right to settle matters They needed means, and everything was there. There was-- had to fix up matters at the factory, had to fix up the home arrangement, everything. Then they went back. And the news we could, that there was no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they left you where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they left me, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remained Toulouse, at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you not go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was a British national. A British citizen at the time. Now, I'm South African. Then it was dangerous for me to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you, at the time, Joseph?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time I was 20-- 24.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had no particular plan. I didn't know. I wanted to get out of France. In fact, [INAUDIBLE] I managed to get a visa. Military age was already a problem at the time, because military age, they were not allowed to leave. The German were controlling, already, France. In the short time they controlled them, France was divided in what they call the Vichy area and occupied France. In the Vichy area, they had, already, to follow some German instructions. And one of these instructions, that military men could not leave the country. In fact, I managed to get a visa for somewhere just to go-- a courtesy visa what was Central America [INAUDIBLE], at the time. But when I wanted to cross the border, they wouldn't allow me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about your parents going back at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at the first stage I wasn't so worried about this, because you used to take information-- information were good. The authorities gave you information saying there's nothing to worry-- behavior of the German is good. And there was nothing anti-Jewish said at the time. Then I wasn't worried. I had a guilt feeling later-- must be honest-- a guilt feeling from a certain letter that I got from my parents. And they seemed to blame me that I couldn't tell them what to do. Now, this was, rather, stage where things were bad. And I couldn't tell them-- well, even if I would have told them. First, mail was censored. You can't write what you want in the mail. And-- and, well, I really thought they should have left Belgium, but I couldn't write so-- And it wasn't easy to leave at the time already. Control was severe. And in fact, some of my family from the States tried to get them out. They booked them, even, a passage from Lisbon on a Portuguese boat to go to the States. They made all the arrangements, but unfortunately they couldn't leave. Officially, they couldn't leave unless they managed to smuggle out of the-- I suppose they couldn't find a way for doing it. Some people managed, some didn't manage. Very few managed to leave the country at that time, because everything was controlled, checked by the Germans. And you used to pay people, you know. There were-- what you call-- people who try to get you out, but this didn't work always. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] In fact, one of my auntie's and their family-- they also arranged with somebody to take them to Switzerland. They were caught on the way.
